analogy: Think of "The Making of a Global World" through the chapter's own opening images: SECTION II LIVELIHOODS, ECONOMIES AND SOCIETIES 53 The Making of a Global World 1 The Pre-modern World When we talk of ‘globalisation’ we often refer to an economic system that has emerged since the last 50 years or so. But as you will see in... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
The Making of a Global World is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that sECTION II LIVELIHOODS, ECONOMIES AND SOCIETIES

The text explains that 53 The Making of a Global World 1 The Pre-modern World When we talk of ‘globalisation’ we often refer to an economic system that has emerged since the last 50 years or so.

The text explains that but as you will see in this chapter, the making of the global world has a long history – of trade, of migration, of people in search of work, the movement of capital, and much else.

The text explains that as we think about the dramatic and visible signs of global interconnectedness in our lives today, we need to understand the phases through which this world in which we live has emerged.

The text explains that all through history, human societies have become steadily more interlinked.

The text explains that from ancient times, travellers, traders, priests and pilgrims travelled vast distances for knowledge, opportunity and spiritual fulfilment, or to escape persecution.

The text explains that they carried goods, money, values, skills, ideas, inventions, and even germs and diseases.

The text explains that as early as 3000 BCE an active coastal trade linked the Indus valley civilisations with present-day West Asia.

The text explains that for more than a millennia, cowries (the Hindi cowdi or seashells, used as a form of currency) from the Maldives found their way to China and East Africa.

The text explains that the long-distance spread of disease-carrying germs may be traced as far back as the seventh century.

The text explains that by the thirteenth century it had become an unmistakable link.

The text explains that the Making of a Global World Chapter III The Making of a Global World Fig.

The text explains that 1 – Image of a ship on a memorial stone, Goa Museum, tenth century CE.

The text explains that from the ninth century, images of ships appear regularly in memorial stones found in the western coast, indicating the significance of oceanic trade.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 54 1.1 Silk Routes Link the World The silk routes are a good example of vibrant pre-modern trade and cultural links between distant parts of the world.

The text explains that the name ‘silk routes’ points to the importance of West-bound Chinese silk cargoes along this route.

The text explains that much before all this, Buddhism emerged from eastern India and spread in several directions through intersecting points on the silk routes.

The text explains that the entry of the Europeans helped expand or redirect some of these flows towards Europe.

The text explains that but from the sixteenth century, its vast lands and abundant crops and minerals began to transform trade and lives everywhere.

The text explains that under pressure from landed groups, the government also restricted the import of corn.

The text explains that nearly 60 per cent of this trade comprised ‘primary products’ – that is, agricultural products such as wheat and cotton, and minerals such as coal.

The text explains that they were directly linked to imperial projects.

The text explains that in the late nineteenth century, Europeans were attracted to Africa due to its vast resources of land and minerals.

The text explains that europeans came to Africa hoping to establish plantations and mines to produce crops and minerals for export to Europe.

The text explains that planters, mine owners and colonial governments now successfully monopolised what scarce cattle resources remained, to strengthen their power and to force Africans into the labour market.

The text explains that control over the scarce resource of cattle enabled European colonisers to conquer and subdue Africa.

The text explains that in fact, the labourers have to spend their period of indenture in great trouble …’ Source: Department of Commerce and Industry, Emigration Branch.

The text explains that industrial investment also grew as the government extended tariff protection to industries, under the pressure of nationalist opinion.

The text explains that at least 60 million people, or about 3 per cent of the world’s 1939 population, are believed to have been killed, directly or indirectly, as a result of the war.

The text explains that they were, however, overburdened by poverty and a lack of resources, and their economies and societies were handicapped by long periods of colonial rule.

The text explains that now, ironically, as newly independent countries facing urgent pressures to lift their populations out of poverty, they came under the guidance of international agencies dominated by the former colonial powers.

The text explains that even after many years of decolonisation, the former colonial powers still controlled vital resources such as minerals and land in many of their former colonies.

The text explains that large corporations of other powerful countries, for example the US, also often managed to secure rights to exploit developing countries’ natural resources very cheaply.

The text explains that by the NIEO they meant a system that would give them real control over their natural resources, more development assistance, fairer prices for raw materials, and better access for their manufactured goods in developed countries’ markets.

The text explains that what is meant by the Bretton Woods Agreement?

The text explains that find out more about gold and diamond mining in South Africa in the nineteenth century.

The text explains that who controlled the gold and diamond companies?

The text explains that who were the miners and what were their lives like?

The text explains that imagine that you are an indentured Indian labourer in the Caribbean.

The text explains that drawing from the details in this chapter, write a letter to your family describing your life and feelings.

The text explains that explain the three types of movements or flows within international economic exchange.

The text explains that find one example of each type of flow which involved India and Indians, and write a short account of it.

The text explains that explain what is referred to as the G-77 countries.

The text explains that in what ways can G-77 be seen as a reaction to the activities of the Bretton Woods twins?

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 3: The Making of a Global World

prelims_traps: Do not treat SECTION II and LIVELIHOODS, ECONOMIES AND SOCIETIES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Making of a Global World; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Making of a Global World through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use SECTION II and LIVELIHOODS, ECONOMIES AND SOCIETIES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
